questions.question_text: Which type of listing guarantees the broker a commission regardless of who finds the buyer?
distractors: Exclusive agency listing
Open listing
Net listing
questions.explanation: An exclusive right to sell listing pays the broker's commission regardless of who sells the property — even if the owner finds the buyer. This is the most common listing type in Ohio.

questions.question_text: A seller has an exclusive agency listing and finds a buyer without any agent's help. Must the seller pay the listing broker's commission?
questions.correct_answer: No, the owner-found buyer is excluded
distractors: Yes, all exclusive listings guarantee commission
Only if the buyer also has an agent
Only if the sale price exceeds the listing price
questions.explanation: Under an exclusive agency listing, the broker earns commission unless the owner sells the property themselves. If the owner independently finds the buyer, no commission is owed.

questions.question_text: Ohio's Statute of Frauds requires listing agreements to be in which form?
distractors: Verbal form with two witnesses
Notarized and recorded form
Any form agreed upon by the parties
questions.explanation: Under the Statute of Frauds, all listing agreements must be in writing. A listing must specify the property, price, commission, dates, broker name, and owner signature.

questions.question_text: A net listing allows the broker to keep everything above the seller's specified minimum price. In Ohio, this listing type is:
distractors: Prohibited by Ohio license law
The most commonly used listing type
Required for commercial properties only
questions.explanation: Net listings are legal in Ohio but discouraged because they create an inherent conflict of interest — the broker benefits by getting the lowest possible price for the seller.

questions.question_text: A seller lists their home with three different brokerages simultaneously. What type of listing agreement does each brokerage hold?
distractors: Exclusive right to sell
Exclusive agency
Net listing
questions.explanation: An open listing allows the owner to list with multiple brokers. Only the broker who actually procures the buyer earns the commission. If the owner sells independently, no commission is owed.

questions.question_text: A listing agent receives a written offer while another offer is already pending. What must the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Present both offers promptly to the seller
distractors: Hold the new offer until the first is resolved
Present only the higher offer to the seller
Reject the new offer as untimely submitted
questions.explanation: Ohio law requires all written offers to be presented promptly to the client, regardless of any pending offers. The seller decides how to respond to each offer.

questions.question_text: A buyer submits an offer of $250,000. The seller counters at $260,000. What happened to the buyer's original offer?
questions.correct_answer: It was rejected by the counteroffer
distractors: It remains active alongside the counter
It becomes a backup offer automatically
It is held open for 48 hours by default
questions.explanation: Under Ohio contract law, a counteroffer rejects the original offer and creates a new offer. The buyer's $250,000 offer is no longer valid once the seller counters at $260,000.

questions.question_text: A purchase contract contains a 'time is of the essence' clause. A buyer misses the inspection deadline by one day. What is the likely result?
questions.correct_answer: The buyer may lose their right to the inspection contingency
distractors: An automatic 3-day extension is granted
The deadline is extended to the next business day
The contract is voided and earnest money returned
questions.explanation: Time is of the essence clauses are strictly enforced in Ohio. Missing deadlines, even by one day, can result in loss of rights or breach of contract.

questions.question_text: A seller instructs their agent in writing not to present offers below $300,000. The agent receives a $280,000 offer. What should the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Follow the seller's written instruction and not present it
distractors: Present it anyway since all offers must be presented
Present it but advise the seller to reject it
Hold it and wait for a higher offer to arrive
questions.explanation: While agents must generally present all offers, Ohio allows sellers to instruct their agent in writing not to present offers meeting specific criteria. Written instructions must be followed.

questions.question_text: Multiple counteroffers are issued on a property. A binding contract is formed when:
questions.correct_answer: The first counteroffer is accepted and communicated
distractors: All counteroffers are simultaneously accepted
The highest counteroffer is automatically accepted
The listing agent selects the winning counteroffer
questions.explanation: When multiple counteroffers exist, the first one accepted creates a binding contract. Acceptance must be communicated to the offeror or their agent to be effective.

questions.question_text: Under Ohio contract law, when does acceptance of an offer occur?
questions.correct_answer: When signed and communicated to the offeror
distractors: When the offeree signs the document
When the earnest money deposit is received
When both agents confirm by phone
questions.explanation: Ohio follows standard contract law: acceptance occurs when the offeree signs the offer and communicates acceptance to the offeror or the offeror's agent.

questions.question_text: Which elements are essential for a valid real estate contract in Ohio?
questions.correct_answer: Offer, acceptance, consideration, capacity, and legality
distractors: Only a signed offer and earnest money deposit
Offer, acceptance, and a written description only
Signatures from all parties and a notary seal
questions.explanation: Ohio follows standard contract law requiring offer, acceptance, consideration, legal capacity, legality of purpose, and meeting of the minds for a valid contract.

questions.question_text: An Ohio broker closes a transaction and stores the file. For how long must all transaction records be retained?
questions.correct_answer: 3 years from closing or listing termination
distractors: 1 year from the transaction date
5 years from the closing date
7 years per federal requirements
questions.explanation: Ohio requires brokers to retain all transaction records — listing agreements, purchase contracts, trust account records, correspondence, and disclosures — for 3 years.

questions.question_text: A listing agreement is signed but the broker loses the copy 18 months later. The listing had terminated without a sale. Must the broker still have this record?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, the 3-year retention period has not expired
distractors: No, records of expired listings are not required
No, only closed transaction records must be kept
Only if the seller requests a copy
questions.explanation: Records must be retained for 3 years from either closing or listing termination. Even if a listing expires without a sale, the record must be kept for the full retention period.

questions.question_text: A buyer signs a purchase agreement but their agent fails to communicate the signed acceptance to the seller's agent. Under Ohio law, is a contract formed?
questions.correct_answer: No, acceptance must be communicated to be effective
distractors: Yes, the buyer's signature alone creates the contract
Yes, if the buyer retains a signed copy
Only if the buyer mails the signed agreement
questions.explanation: Mere signing is insufficient — acceptance must be communicated to the offeror or their agent. Without communication, no binding contract exists under Ohio contract law.
